The '''New Caledonia men's national football team''' ({{langx|fr|équipe de Nouvelle-Calédonie de football}}), nicknamed '''{{lang|fr|Les Cagous}}''' ({{translation|"The [[Kagu]]s"}}), is the national team of [[New Caledonia]]; it is controlled by the [[Fédération Calédonienne de Football]]. Although they were only admitted to [[FIFA]] in 2004, they have been participating in the [[OFC Nations Cup]] since its inception. They have been one of this relatively small region's strongest teams, finishing second in [[2008 OFC Nations Cup|2008]] and [[2012 OFC Nations Cup|2012]], and third in [[1973 OFC Nations Cup|1973]] and [[1980 OFC Nations Cup|1980]]. They were the top ranked OFC nation at number 95 in September 2008,<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20070603154230/http://www.fifa.com/worldfootball/ranking/lastranking/gender=m/fullranking.html#confederation=23916&rank=173 FIFA/Coca-Cola World Ranking September 2008]</ref> making them only the fourth country from the confederation to have reached the global top 100.

==History==

The [[New Caledonian Football Federation]], although created in 1928,<ref>{{cite web |title=A Little History |url=https://www.fedcalfoot.com/Un-peu-d-histoire_r14.html |website=Federation of Caledonian Football |access-date=1 February 2022 |archive-date=1 February 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220201135851/https://www.fedcalfoot.com/Un-peu-d-histoire_r14.html |url-status=live }}</ref> did not join [[FIFA]] or the [[Oceania Football Confederation|OFC]] until 2004, becoming the 205th member of the former.

Previously the New Caledonian selection, due to the attachment of local institutions to the [[French Football Federation]], could only line up during friendly matches or regional competitions, such as the Pacific Games, as well as the Oceania Football Cup, but only as a guest.

The ''Cagous'' distinguished themselves during these competitions, winning the Pacific Games on several occasions, and finishing in third place, during the first two editions of the [[OFC Nations Cup]].

After failing to qualify for the [[2004 OFC Nations Cup]], the [[New Caledonian Football Federation]] hired [[Didier Chambaron]] as the team's new coach. During the [[2007 South Pacific Games]] in Apia, the ''Cagous'' were placed in group A and won over their great rival [[Tahiti national football team|Tahiti]] (1–0), this meeting was the first of the playoffs for the [[2010 FIFA World Cup]]. They then won against [[Tuvalu national football team|Tuvalu]] and the [[Cook Islands national football team|Cook Islands]] (1–0) and (3–0). For their last group match, they drew against [[Fiji national football team|Fiji]] (1–1). The New Caledonians finished second in their group, and then beat the [[Solomon Islands national football team|Solomon Islands]] (3–2), then in the final won the trophy against Fiji (1–0).

The Pacific Games football tournament serving as the first qualifying round for the World Cup in South Africa, the team found themselves qualified for the second round, where [[New Zealand men's national football team|New Zealand]] blocked their way by taking first place. The ''Cagous'' however finish second in front of [[Fiji national football team|Fiji]] and [[Vanuatu national football team|Vanuatu]].

In May 2011, the selection faced [[Reunion national football team|Reunion]] twice in [[Nouméa]], matches counting for the preparation of the games of the islands of the Indian Ocean in the Seychelles of the Reunionese. The New Caledonians lost both matches. However, a few months later, during the [[2011 Pacific Games]], New Caledonia won in the final against the [[Solomon Islands national football team|Solomon Islands]], with the score of (2–0), after a victory against Tahiti (3–1).

In June 2012, on the occasion of the [[OFC Nations Cup]], the ''Cagous'' succeeded in beating [[New Zealand men's national football team|New Zealand]] in the semi-finals, with a score of 2–0 (goals from [[Bertrand Kai]] and [[Georges Gope-Fenepej]]), but failed in the final against their Tahitian rivals (1–0). Nevertheless, the competition serving as a second qualifying round for the [[2014 FIFA World Cup]], New Caledonia found itself, along with Tahiti and the semi-finalists New Zealand and the Solomon Islands qualified for the next round.

==Rivalries==
A historical sporting rivalry exists between the two French Pacific overseas collectivities, New Caledonia and [[Tahiti national football team|Tahiti]]. They compete regularly in regional and, since 2006, international competitions. In 2012, Tahiti led the number of titles won (1 [[OFC Nations Cup]], 5 gold medals at the [[South Pacific Games]], 2 at the South Pacific mini-games, against 6 gold at the South Pacific Games for New Caledonia). As of their last match in 2018, out of the 62 matches played since 1953, the New Caledonia has 28 wins against 25 for Tahiti and 9 draws.{{citation needed|date=January 2025}}

==Kit==
[[Macron (sportswear)|Macron]] are the current kit provider for the national team since 2025. As of 2012, the national team's home kit is a grey jersey with red shorts and red socks. The away kit is a red jersey with red shorts and white socks.

{| class="wikitable" style="text-align:left;"

! Period

==Coaching history==
{{div col|colwidth=22em}}
*{{flagicon|France|1830}} [[Guy Elmour]] (1971–1973)
*{{flagicon|France|1974}} [[Jules Hmeune]] (1977)
*{{flagicon|West Germany}} [[Rudi Gutendorf]] (1981)
*{{flagicon|New Caledonia|french|1974}} [[Jacques Zimako]]{{efn|name=NewCaledonia-France-only-flag|New Caledonia [[FIFA eligibility rules|sporting nationality]]. At the time, no other flag other than the [[French flag]] was used}} (1995)
*{{flagicon|France|1974}} [[Michel Clarque]] (2002)
*{{flagicon|France|1974}} [[Serge Martinengo de Novack]] (2002–2004)
*{{flagicon|France|1974}} [[Didier Chambaron]] (2007–2010)
*{{flagicon|France|1974}} [[Christophe Coursimault]] (2010–2012)
*{{flagicon|France|1974}} [[Alain Moizan]] (2012–2015)
*{{flagicon|France}} [[Thierry Sardo]] (2015–2021)
*{{flagicon|NCL|football}} [[Dominique Wacalie]] (2021–2022)
*{{flagicon|France}} [[Johann Sidaner]] (2022–2026)
{{div col end}}

==Players==
===Current squad===

*<sup>INJ</sup> Withdrew due to injury
*<sup>PRE</sup> Preliminary squad / standby
*<sup>WD</sup> Withdrew due to non-injury issue
{{nat fs end}}

|win_VAN=25|draw_VAN=7 |loss_VAN=8 |gf_VAN=102|ga_VAN=45
|win_WAF=3 |draw_WAF=0 |loss_WAF=0 |gf_WAF=19 |ga_WAF=1

|update=complete|source=[[New Caledonia national football team results (1951–1999)#Results|1951–99 results]] and [[New Caledonia national football team results (2000–present)#Results|2000–present results]]
}}

==Honours==
===Continental===
* '''[[OFC Nations Cup]]'''
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (2): [[2008 OFC Nations Cup|2008]], [[2012 OFC Nations Cup|2012]]
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (2): [[1973 OFC Nations Cup|1973]], [[1980 OFC Nations Cup|1980]]

===Regional===
* '''[[Football at the Pacific Games|Pacific Games]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Gold medal (7):''' [[1963 Pacific Games |1963]], [[1969 South Pacific Games |1969]], [[1971 South Pacific Games |1971]], [[1987 South Pacific Games |1987]], [[2007 South Pacific Games |2007]], [[2011 Pacific Games |2011]], [[2023 Pacific Games|2023]]
** {{Silver2}} Silver medal (4): [[1966 Pacific Games |1966]], [[1987 South Pacific Games |1987]], [[2003  Pacific Games |2003]], [[2019  Pacific Games |2019]]
** {{Bronze3}} Bronze medal (2): [[1983 Pacific Games |1983]], [[1991 South Pacific Games |1991]]
* '''[[MSG Prime Minister's Cup|Melanesia Cup / MSG Prime Minister's Cup]]'''
** {{Silver2}} Runner-up (4): [[1989 Melanesia Cup|1989]], [[1990 Melanesia Cup|1990]], [[1992 Melanesia Cup|1992]], [[2023 MSG Prime Minister's Cup|2023]]

===Awards===
* '''[[OFC Nations Cup awards#Fair Play Award|OFC Nations Cup Fair Play Award]]''': 2016

===Summary===
{| class="wikitable" width=30% style="font-size:90%; text-align:center;"
|-
!Competition !!{{gold1}} !!{{silver2}} !!{{bronze3}} !!Total
|-
|align=left|[[OFC Nations Cup]]
|0||2||2||4
|-
!Total!!0!!2!!2!!4
|}
